speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the five [INAUDIBLE] I could hardly move. We got assigned a barrack. David and I slept into one bunk. Jackie slept with somebody else in a bunk. And the next morning, off to work we go. That's-- that's what it was. What the Auschwitz whole system was, Auschwitz I was the main camp, with about 24,000 people at all times. Birkenau was number two, with about 34,000. Buna-Monowitz had about 13,000 to 14,000. So you had a total of about 170-- you had about a total of 70,000 people at all times.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does a camp like this get directed? How does it operate? It operates like a prison. You have the hierarchy, which were the German criminals, the [GERMAN], the professional criminals, or the lifers. Then you had the politicals, with the red triangle, who were the kapos and the block chiefs. They were directing the traffic.

speaker: interviewee
text: The trouble was that the German criminals were Germans. They were not Jews. They hated the Jews just as much as the Germans did. So life was miserable. The-- eventually, in 1944, that changed. The political communistic element took over. The green triangles, the professional criminal element, were shoved out. So it was advantageous to us, in as  much as they didn't like the Jews either. But they treated us better. For the most part, you had Jews in there, it was just one of those things. By 5 o'clock in the morning, again, you got woken up, just like in the first camp, same thing. The only difference was that you got your coffee and your bread in the morning. You cleaned yourself with cold water. You couldn't drink the water. Auschwitz was a swamp. You could only be boiling water. You drank the water; within a half an hour, you were dead. You had colic, and you were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by 6 o'clock, you formed commandos, or working units. And you had like 60 blocks, 60 barracks. Each barrack had about 182 people in it. So figure on the people walking out to work to Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did in Buna was synthetic rubber and [INAUDIBLE], industry, Bayer. They made synthetic medicines, like the Bayer aspirins and all that. This is what they did, or we did. And then you watched out with music, to march music. Of course, the inmates, they were an orchestra. And as you came by the entrance gate to walk out, you had two SS on each side, and the block chief, and the commando chief from all the commandos-- lagerkapo. And they had all rubber hoses. And as you had to take off your cap, they looked on top of your head because you had hair. If your head was dirty, you got a slug on your head. Usually, they had steel inside the rubber hose. By the time these six guys hit you on the head, either you had a headache, or you didn't feel too well, one of the two. That meant clean your head the next time. And coming home the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had about a half an hour to go to work, 3/4 of an hour, and you started at 7 o'clock to work, whatever you did-- digging ditches, electrical work, whatever your commando did. And by 12:00, the siren rang. You got your lunch, which was about a quart of soup or dishwater with chicory, whatever you wanted to call it-- with a few leaves in it. And then at 1 o'clock, you went back to work. And by 5 o'clock, the siren rang.

speaker: interviewee
text: You formed your commando, marched back to the camp. Inside the camp, you went back to the big place. You were counted by barrack because the commando dismantled. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if the count was right, you were dismissed. You went to your barrack, cleaned yourself, got your quart of soup for the night. That's what you've got, a little [INAUDIBLE]. And then by 9 o'clock was curfew, and you had to go to sleep. And there was a bucket there in the middle of the room, where you went for your duties at night. You couldn't go out of the barrack. If the bucket was full, and you went there, and the chief of the-- the-- the night chief saw you, you had to carry the damn thing out to the latrines, which is one of those things. You were elected. So, of course, you tried to avoid that. You became wise to this. In between the sleeping time and the dinner time, if you had a slice of bread, and you wanted three cigarettes, you went to the black marketeering, and you started handling and bargaining there. You got about 250 grams of bread in the morning. And once a week, you got 20 grams of oleo, margarine, and one spoon of jam. Hey. According to the caloric figures by the SS, within three months, you were gone. So what you had to do is you had to scrounge. You had to get contacts. You had to know people who gave you an extra ladle of soup, an extra this. And if you knew people-- and I knew people from the first camp, and I knew people from Berlin, and we were all in the same hole. Because out of 100 people coming in on a Monday, by about Saturday, you have about 30 left. And these 30 will stay. And out of the 30, maybe later on, five, six will stay alive. That was a rule. This is a policy. Because first of all, you didn't have enough food. You get skinny so far, and that's the end of the melody. If you can sustain that, you're all right. If you can't that's too bad. Many people died emotionally because their heart was broken. They were too old. They couldn't sustain it. And you become numb. You become like an automat, like a robot. You do things automatically. You forget where you are. You forget the outside because there is no hope. There is nothing there. You get up. You go to bed. You get up. You go to bed. You pray. You try to stay alive. That's it. Whenever a truck came and picked up the cadavers or picked up the people, then you knew already they were gone. To give you an example, we came in-- I was there two months. We came in in an evening. And the count was on an appellplatz, what they called appellplatz, an assemblement place, and two fellows were missing. It's cold. In Auschwitz in a good wintertime, it's 40 below. No snow. It blows wind, and you had no clothing. So we stay, and we wait, and we wait, and we wait. By about 10 o'clock, you hear the commandant speaking over the speaker system, gentlemen, we're sorry. Two of the fellows didn't want to leave the town. They didn't come home with us. We need to wait for them. We waited till 5 o'clock in the morning, which the gong rang, we went back to work. 3,000 men died that night. In my whole total two whole years camp time in Auschwitz, I spent 10 nights like this. When I came back, I had frozen feet, frozen hands, frozen earlobes, which is one of those things. To give you a better example, about four months later, they had a selection. It's called the [NON-ENGLISH]. Too many youngsters in the camp, so they took 500 youngsters between 14 and 19, we undressed. There was a chief doctor, the SS man. And you had to parade in front of him. And fate was his life was all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jackie went in front of me and was a pretty husky little guy. He was OK. He was older than me, on top of it. And I came, and I looked like, you know, who's he? And he looked at me, and at that split the moment, the German in me came out. I stood in attention, gave him my number in-- I mean, as military German as you can have. When I speak German militarily, it sounds very frightening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he looked at me, he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], where are you from? And I said, Berlin. Berlin. He says, ah, born in Berlin? Born in Berlin? He says, [INAUDIBLE]. Hmm, [SPEAKING GERMAN], go on to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going to starve and die in two weeks anyway. What made him do it? I have no idea. Fate, no room upstairs, no room down-- I have no idea. Don't ask me. That saved me. Out of the 300 youngsters-- of the 500 youngsters, 300 went into trucks into the gas. We were 200 staying behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: By 1945, when we were liberated, out of the 200 youngsters, five of us came back. That gives you a percentage. Jackie, myself, a little Russian guy-- I don't know the two other ones were. I saw them once, but that's about it. That gives you a percentage. By the time 1944 came about, like, I-- I went in January into the infirmary with double pneumonia. Dr. Weiss, who was very nice, and Professor Samarides was very nice. They said, Louis, you can't stay here. They're going to come every two weeks, and they're going to pick you up. The way you are, they're-- you're going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So uncured double pneumonia. I went out again for a whole year. By the time December came around, even with two friends I had to work with me all the time-- David, in the meantime, had passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Noah and Yannik were my buddies. I couldn't-- I couldn't even walk. I couldn't even crawl anymore. I couldn't breathe. I was gone. I was just on the end of the melody. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to get out and walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me to the infirmary. Dr. Weiss gave me one look, took a test on me and says, you're tuberculosis positive, Louis. You've going to have to stay here-- on both lungs. And on the 15th of January, they started marching out of Auschwitz, the famous death march. Noah and Yannik came and says, Louis, come. We'll carry you. We'll put you on a  sled. I says, Dr. Samarides told me to stay here. Nothing will happen to me. And I stayed behind. That was my fortune because I would have never made it. I couldn't even crawl anymore. I was 5' 4' by this-- I grew in the camp two inches. And I weighed 52 pounds. And I was spitting and coughing forever and ever, every minute of the time. By the 27th of January at 6 o'clock in the morning, the first 10 Russians came into the camp. And we saw the first Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the question always is asked of me, how did you feel? Joy? What joy? I was so numb. I didn't know from Adam. I had no thoughts. I just looked at the Russians. I said, this is it. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took me two weeks to understand logically, intellectually what is going on, what happened. And I saw the Russians, I mean, putting quick lye on because of the-- the-- the bodies they couldn't keep and whatever have you. That's about what it amounts to. Then I was sent to the main camp because they dissolved Auschwitz III. And there, the Russian military, the-- the-- the medical court took over. I have pictures of them. And they took care of us. I got artificial air in the lungs and so on. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take good care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very good care. Very good care. And in the meantime, Auschwitz was made a camp for prisoners of war for the German prisoners of war. There was 70,000 going every day through via Siberia and whatever have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were just in two, little, tiny blocks, and that was it. Unfortunately for us, I met an old friend of mine, Abram Slavinsky, out of Antwerp, and Abram, Agrit, Tiko, Aholande, Yapi and I, by the time June came around, we went out on our own because we'd had no Red Cross picking us up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Krakow. We stayed there for-- for a week under care in a hospital. Then we went, illegally again, from the Polish border to Czechoslovakia. We spent a week in Czechoslovakia in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautifully, by foot, walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. By this time, I was-- hey, I had already food. The Russians-- hey, I was already  on top of the world again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were healthy by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god, yes. I needed the food. That's what it was. And the French Red Cross picked us up. I changed my name in Prague so that they don't get an idea that I was a German. My French was so fluent and so French. I said I was a Belgian. [SPEAKING FRENCH] With the name of Louis Posner. My name originally is Louis Posener. So that's where this comes in. And they sent-- they flew us to Pilsen in Germany and then into the Black Forest, where the French occupation was, into a sanatorium. And we got served well. We began to live again. That's when we all came together. Abram was there. Tiko was there. My-- I made friends over there from Belgium. So and-- by April '46, I was perfect. It was terrific. I never went into a DP camp. The doctor who was captain, [INAUDIBLE], who is still alive in Paris, I corresponded with.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says. Louis, I'm going to another sanatorium. How would you like to come with me? You can be the mailman six months out of the year and the economist the other six months. Get you a little money, hey, you're still in a sanatorium. You don't have to worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did. And by the year of '47, end of '47, the [INAUDIBLE] took over, and they provided a room in the village, and I stayed there. And I think two months later, one man from the [INAUDIBLE] came and says, Mr. Posner, you will need to learn a profession in order to immigrate. You can't stay in Germany. There's no jobs. I says, and I don't want to stay in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I did is I went to the University of Tubingen, learned some kind of physical therapy there, and went back again. And by the time 1949 came around, I went into another camp, a rehabilitation camp, in-- in [INAUDIBLE] in Germany. And there I started my papers. I got married there. I started my papers. How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked in an office in the camp. And we got married, and by 1951, we migrated to the United States. We were allowed to come in. We had a visa, a regular quota. And I found out in the between time that my dad in 19-- was sent to [INAUDIBLE], then to Gurs, and in Gurs he spent till 1942. In about in May '42, he was set to the prison of [INAUDIBLE], then to the camp [INAUDIBLE] into [INAUDIBLE]. Got deported in September '42 to Auschwitz, to the main camp, spent a week in a bunker. I have no idea why. I've got to go to Auschwitz in order to find out why. Was sent to Auschwitz III, where I was later on, and became with the underground, [INAUDIBLE], the famous Austrian underground men. And then by December, it was all over. How he passed away, how he died, I have no idea. I'd never seen him since he left. My mother, in 1941, November '41, was the first transport out of Berlin, going towards Latvia. They arrived at 6 o'clock in the morning, about 1,009 people, and they were shot at-- on  the spot, all of them. I found that out-- you will never believe it-- I found it out three months ago. I got a book sent from Berlin. I have a publisher in Berlin, a bookstore, who always has books, all kinds, and he sends me always if I want this, and if I want this, and I send him some money, and he sends me the book. And that book is called [SPEAKING GERMAN]. This is where they were deported from, from Grunewald, in Berlin, near Berlin. And that's where I found it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You found her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't-- I-- her name is in one of the books. But I found out what happened to the transport. I know exactly which transport she went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, also, from a book, but also from papers. I have documents. See, once I started writing my book in '83, it took me a good-- from '79 to '83 research. I wrote to every government in Germany, Belgium, and France and Poland to get papers and documents. And I got answers. My home, my room in my home now is like a museum. I have nothing but-- like you saw this morning-- nothing but catalogs full of documents. So whatever I tell in a story to youngsters, to people, or in the book, it's all documented with pictures and papers by governmental agencies. That's the way I found it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you think, after the war, when you were starting to heal, how did you start to live again?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I have never hated anybody in my life. And if I did, it was wrong. Finally, when I came back, in the time between going to America and being alone, growing up, growing pains and going up, I learned with Abram. We talked. We talked Talmud. We talked about all kinds of philosophies. And we came to the agreement that hate will only destroy. We had no love for the Germans. Who ever talked to us, we didn't talk to them. But hating a baby who was born in 1947 didn't have anything to do with the wartime-- can't. So I've grown in my years, in my own way of thinking, that I don't hate anybody or anything. I won't forget. It will always be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will never, never go back to live there. I mean, I've been asked that question. And visiting the country, oh, yeah-- it's a gorgeous country. But who needs to talk to the people? I don't talk to the people. I stay with the group if I go someplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I visited my mother's grave, who's still in Berlin, the first mother, who was buried. Finally, I saw it. I saw it twice now. And it's interesting how mechanically a man becomes. The first time, it was emotion. I said, hi, mom. And I really didn't know what to do. I cleaned the grave and all this. The second time eh. It wasn't there anymore. When I came home in '79 from 28 days in-- in Europe, visiting my friends in the places where I've been, I went there as a-- as a man to look for the childhood he had spent. And I came into Europe like-- like a foreigner. But still, this is my home. When I came home again, after the 28 days at the airport, my son and my wife was there, and I says, gee, it's nice to be home. That's all I could say at midnight. The second time I went, I went with her, because we were invited by the Congress in Berlin. But I went as an American visiting another country, going on a speaking system, and I felt entirely different.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came home after, again, 24, 25 days, because I took her to Belgium to my friends and so on, we spent some money on our own, because seven days in Berlin was free. When we came home again, she looked at me and says, well? I says, who needs it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the way I felt about it-- who needs it. I have no need for it. It's completely gone. I am an American. This is my home. I was even inducted in the army here. So, you know, this is-- this is my place. This is what I will fight and-- and-- and live for. And so will my son. This is what it amounts to. What is over there is past history. I look up on the period from 1933 to '45 as a very bad time in my life. But I can't live in that time. I live today, and I live better tomorrow. I live today. This is past. I will only remember and talk about it and teach that it may not happen again. But I can't live in the past. That will destroy me, and it never has. So that's the way I formed my life. I came here to America in 1951 in September, and don't tell me it's easy, because it ain't. I worked for $1 an hour six days a week to pay the rent. I mean, you moved up, and you moved up, and you moved up, and it got better, and it got better, and it got better. And I learned to love this country. I learned to love the people, their simplicity or not simplicity.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have very few friends and very few acquaintances. I have lived my life. And the question always becomes also, why does a man like me talk to youngsters, to high schools? Is it hurting me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, heavens, no-- it's making me feel good. It's power. It's energy. To teach these kids is terrific. They're a good audience. They need to know. They need to be aware. If they're not aware, it's going to happen tomorrow morning, in this country, what happened 50 years ago. It is not any different. All you have to do is go to the Midwest into the woods, the paramilitary, the skinheads. It's all resurging. Economies go sour, the Hitlers come up again. This is what we need to tell the youngsters, to avoid this, that they are aware, get involved. I give them an example. If they go out of the class onto the school ground, and there are 10 youngsters in a round, and one boy is raping a girl, I give you an educated guess no one will interfere. That's what's wrong. You need to get involved. You need to interfere. You need to speak up because that's a way to avoid. And there is no comparison between Rwanda, Somalia, Russia now, Yugoslavia, that's civil wars. That's not killing a people because they're Jews. One thing has nothing to do with the other. In as much as I deplore our silence, I deplore what we're doing. And I have learned over my life that the CIA, they're just as bad as the [GERMAN] in Germany. It is not any different. It's just in a different country. The killing they did in South America, it's the same thing. And the corruptness in this country at this stage of the game is so bad. This is what's going to kill us. If you look in history-- and I'm a buff in history, and I've studied it-- you look at history, whatever killed a country is corruptness and greed. We're doing it. All we have to listen is to Clinton. We have to listen to Gingrich to Dole. If you follow this politics, that's where it's at-- not any different, just is a different era. That's all it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I just want to get back to something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've-- you've talked so much about how you survived so much on the street and everything. Do you think that those same survival skills are the ones that helped you survive Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't think so. No, I don't think so. I think what makes people survive is a basic biology. You know, you're-- you're made out of a certain chemistry. 50% is genes, which you have no control over. Then you have about 30% which is your make up and so on. And the other 20% is what you make up in your life, your philosophy, what you form. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My streetwise-ness had nothing to do with Auschwitz. Auschwitz and the streetwise kids, they were not identical because people have the mannerism of saying, the intellectuals couldn't stand. They got killed. It was the thugs and the hoods who survived, which is not really true. There were many, many intellectuals who survived, many doctors, many professors who survived, many writers. That's not really true. On the street, maybe the hoods survived and the intellectuals go broke. But it is your make up, your genes which makes you. And that's what makes you survive. And it is your attitude, the attitude you take to whatever is. I came into Auschwitz and says, hey, I'm going to get out of here. It's going to be a little while, hey. If I wake up in the morning and breathe regularly, everything is lovely. That's the way you look at it. And when the end comes, the end comes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's just one of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't think one thing has anything to do with the other. You know, I've been looked over by psychologists, by psychiatrists, what makes me tick, because I wake up happy, and I go to bed happy. I've never had any problems in my lifetime. I don't have any problems. Whatever comes along, hey, it's terrific. If I don't like it, I look the other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't like me, look the other way. You don't have to like me. I don't have to like it. So it's a-- a-- a-- it's an attitude toward life you take. And this is what I've done. In Auschwitz, in my childhood, in my later years in America, that's what I've done. And that's where it's all at. There is nothing else what makes a tick. And I mean, I've talked Eli Wiesel, and I've talked to all these people. I-- I'm not a religious man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? You came from a very traditional home. What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I don't believe in organized religion. I just don't. I never have, and I never will.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that come--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's a following of the-- let me give you the best example where my turn-off comes in. My turn-off comes in that when-- at one time, when the Hungarian transports came-- you're from them-- they had barracks. They had [INAUDIBLE]. They had [INAUDIBLE], for some reason, to do the morning prayer, which was fine. In Auschwitz, the biggest crime, when you stole something from somebody else, when you stole a piece of bread, you were a criminal. If they caught you, they clubbed you to death on the spot-- on the spot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They [INAUDIBLE] in the morning. They prayed very religiously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then about 10 minutes later, I see one of the guys going into the barrack, and I follow him. And he goes to a comrade under the mattress and steals his bread. This is what religion teaches you? Your comrade is just as hungry as you are. That's what I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going to church, going to synagogues, going to mosque and not believing in religion is silly Going once a year on the high holidays doesn't make you a Jew, not as far as I'm concerned. The mitzvahs, the good deeds make you a Jew. You helping your people, that's what makes you a Jew, not going to the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I don't believe in organized religion. I believe in something superior and something higher, definitely. But as far as religion, this is a business to me. I can't afford $400 to go to Yom Kippur in synagogues. Forget it, and I'm not about to. If I want to talk to God, if I want to talk to anything, I stay in the corner, facing east, and taught to him very nicely. And I get answers. It's terrific. So that's the way I feel about it. And I know I've lost friends over this. I've lost acquaintances over this because they say, yeah, you're a heathen and all. That doesn't bother me. Whatever they-- you know, what people think of me has no consequence to me. I know what I'm worth. I know what I look like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can look in a mirror and see my soul, and that's all that matters. I've learned to see it. I love it. It's terrific. I'm a tremendous human being, and that's the way I feel about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all there is to it.
